title: Bengali Machher Jhol

subs.name: 600 g paneer cubes for a vegetarian version
subs.notes: fry paneer for 2 minutes only; it needs far less simmer time

subs.name: raw banana wedges
subs.notes: add 2 extra minutes to the simmering step as raw banana takes longer to soften

subs.name: any neutral cooking oil
subs.notes: mustard oil is the signature aroma; refined oil produces a milder curry

steps.text: Rub the fish steaks all over with half the turmeric and a generous pinch of salt. Leave to marinate for 10 minutes while you chop the tomatoes and cut the potatoes.

Heat mustard oil in a kadai over high flame until it turns pale and the sharp raw smell mellows. Carefully lower the fish pieces in and shallow-fry until golden and firm on both sides, about 3 minutes per side. Remove and set on a plate.

Reduce to medium flame. Add the kalonji and slit green chillies to the same oil and let them splutter for 20 seconds. Add the potato wedges and fry, turning occasionally, until lightly golden on the cut sides, about 3 minutes.
Add the tomatoes, remaining turmeric, red chilli powder and ginger paste. Stir well and cook on medium until the tomatoes collapse and the oil rises to the surface, about 4 minutes.
Pour in 400 ml water and season with salt. Bring to a rolling boil, then cover and simmer on medium until the potatoes are just tender when pierced with a knife, about 8 minutes.
Slide the fried fish pieces into the gravy. Simmer uncovered for 5 minutes, spooning the gravy over the fish without breaking the steaks. The jhol should remain thin and golden.
Taste for salt, scatter coriander leaves over the top, and rest 5 minutes before serving. Ladle the jhol generously over hot steamed rice and eat immediately.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
Keep water away from hot oil, lower food gently, avoid overfilling the vessel and never leave frying unattended.
critical_checks: Cook seafood until safely done for the species and preparation; keep it chilled before cooking.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: fish → 600 g paneer cubes for a vegetarian version (fry paneer for 2 minutes only; it needs far less simmer time); mustard-oil → any neutral cooking oil (mustard oil is the signature aroma; refined oil produces a milder curry); potato → raw banana wedges (add 2 extra minutes to the simmering step as raw banana takes longer to soften)
